segments.s636-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. زین آگهی is an archaic/poetic phrase meaning 'this news/announcement' (زین = زِ این, 'of this/this'), شد is the past/perfective marker 'happened/became' meaning the news 'came' or 'was made known', and به + noun marks the indirect object 'to the Faghfur (emperor) of China'. Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal clause → indirect object.
segments.s636-g2: که introduces a content clause/relative 'that/which' reporting what the news was. با فر is an instrumental/qualificative phrase 'with/possessing farr (royal glory)'. مردی is the indefinite noun 'a man'. ز marks source 'from' in poetic register, and ایران‌زمین is the compound 'land of Iran'. The clause gives the content of the news.
segments.s636-g3: به نزدیک + place = 'to the vicinity of' (نزدیک governing a locative sense). فرستاده here is a perfective participle/past form 'sent' (participle used in a compound past), and بود is the past auxiliary 'had been/was', forming a past perfective sense: 'had been sent' or 'was sent'.
segments.s636-g4: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/indeed' that introduces an assertion. ز ایران is the poetic 'from Iran' (ز = from), تهم‌زاده is a patronymic/epithet 'Tahm‑zadeh' (son/descendant of Tahm), and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause asserts the man's origin/lineage.
segments.s636-g5: بدو is the unstressed prepositional phrase 'to him' (بِ + او contracted), placing the indirect object first for emphasis. داد is the past tense verb 'gave'. شنگل is the giver's name; یکی دخترش is 'one (a certain) of his daughters', where یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one/a certain' and دخترش 'his daughter'. Word order is indirect object → verb → subject → object (poetic flexibility).
segments.s636-g6: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'دخترش' ('who/whose'). بر is the preposition 'upon/on'. ماه is used as a simile/metaphor ('the moon' or 'beautiful one' depending on context); here it figures in a simile. ساید is an archaic/poetic verb/adjective in this line and governs the clause's predicate (sense is uncertain in the supplied lexicon). همی is the old continuous/emphatic particle 'ever/always' used here to mark habitual or vivid description. افسرش (افسر + possessive) means 'her diadem/helmet/crown' (a poetic term for head‑jewelry). The entire relative clause describes the daughter's diadem shining like the moon.
segments.s636-g7: یکی here is the indefinite 'a certain' (qualifying نامه: 'a certain letter' or simply 'a letter'). نامه is 'letter'. نزديك in this position is adverbial/locative but in older poetry it can mark direction or closeness to the addressee; syntactically this cluster forms the object/recipient phrase tied to the verb نوشت that follows (the precise nuance is poetic). بهرامشاه is the addressee's name.
segments.s636-g8: نوشت is the simple past 'wrote' (third person). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the', here pointing to the subject. جهاندار is an honorific/epithet 'world‑ruler' or 'sovereign' acting as subject, and با + دستگاه ('with his دستگاه') means 'with/attended by his court/establishment' (با + noun marks accompaniment). The clause states who sent/wrote the letter and that he was accompanied by his retinue.
segments.s636-g57: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' placed before the noun for emphasis; اختر (star) is the noun-predicate and شاه بهرام is an appositive identification. The clause ends with the past copula بود, so the whole segment reads literally 'that same star was King Bahram.'
segments.s636-g58: که introduces a relative clause modifying the star ('who/that'). با governs a series of nouns (qualities) joined by و; each noun is listed as something the king 'had' or was endowed with. بانام is the fused prepositional phrase 'با + نام' (= 'with a name' / 'renown'). The clause closes again with the past copula بود.
segments.s636-g59: This is a nominal statement: هنر (skill) is the topic, نیز 'also/likewise' adds emphasis, and ز + ایرانیانست (here written fused as ایرانیانست) means 'is of the Iranians' or 'belongs to the Iranians' (older/poetic z for 'from/of'). و بس is a brief clause-closer meaning 'and that suffices' or 'and that's enough.'
segments.s636-g60: ندارند is the negative present/past-form 'they do not have/possess' (here generalizing). The direct object (marked by را) is کرگ ژیان, placed before the object-marker in poetic word-order; به کس ('for/to anyone') indicates the beneficiary/reciprocal sense. Overall: 'they do not have کرگ ژیان for anyone.'
segments.s636-g61: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject and یکدلانند is a predicative adjective with the plural present/past copular ending (-ند) meaning 'they are one‑hearted/united.' The coordinating و links a second predicate یزدان‑شناس ('God‑knowing' / 'pious person'), so both descriptions apply to the same people.
segments.s636-g62: به نیکی is an adverbial phrase 'properly/well' modifying ندارند; سپاس is the noun 'thanks/praise' and appears at the end as the thing they lack. The preposition ز (here 'from/of') plus اختر ('star') marks the source/object of the thanks: 'they do not, properly, have thanks from/to the star' = 'they do not properly give thanks to the star.'
segments.s636-g63: دگر آنک opens a new point ('another thing, that...'). The clause that follows has an inverted, poetic word order: object (دختر) + indirect object (به من) + verb (داد) + subject (شاه). Literally: 'Another, that the king gave the daughter to me' (i.e. 'the king gave his daughter to me').
segments.s636-g64: This clause continues the narrative with گرفتم 'I took/received' as the main verb (first person past). به مردی indicates the beneficiary/recipient 'to a man' (here: 'I married her to a man'), and چنین پیشگاه ('in this manner/before the court') is an adverbial phrase describing how/where the action occurred. The sequence shows a direct object understood from the previous clause (the daughter) omitted for brevity.
segments.s636-g9: به عنوان is a fixed classical phrase meaning “as” or “in the capacity/title of.” Here it opens a prepositional/agent phrase. بر از شهریار جهان is a poetic ordering: بر (a preposition-like element) appears before از + noun to indicate agency/origin (“by/from the sovereign of the world”). The whole segment is an introductory prepositional phrase that modifies an implied predicate (the copula is omitted).
segments.s636-g10: This is a nominal/title cluster with no copula: سر (here = “head/chief”) + نامداران (the renowned ones) in apposition, then و شاه مهان (“and king of the مهان”). مهان is used poetically for the great/noble people (literally “guests/visitors” in the lexicon given). The sequence lists two honorific titles applied to the addressee; Persian frequently omits the verb “is/are” in such appositive title strings.
segments.s636-g11: به نزد is a classical locative expression meaning “to the presence of / to” (به + نزد). فرستاده پارسی is a noun phrase “Persian envoy”; پارسی here qualifies فرستاده (a person from Persia). The whole segment is a prepositional phrase indicating the addressee/location.
segments.s636-g12: که is the relative pronoun “who/that.” آمد is the past tense “came.” به قنوج = “to Kannauj” (place-marking). با یار سی means “with thirty companions”; note classical Persian often places the numeral after the counted noun (یار سی = ‘thirty companions’). Word order is subject/relative + verb + directional phrase + accompaniment.
segments.s636-g13: دگر گفت = “he said further / again”; دگر (= again/further) modifies گفت (here functioning as reported speech marker). کامد is an archaic/persianized present/future form meaning “will come / comes” (classical usage), and بما آگهی = “news/notice to us.” The verb (کامد) precedes the indirect object phrase, typical flexibility of classical Persian word order in reported speech.
segments.s636-g14: ز (poetic variant of از) introduces the source/content: “about/of you.” تو is the genitive/person targeted. نامور مرد = “a famed / renowned man.” با فرهی = “with nobility” — با here attaches an adjectival quality (possessing/characterized by), and فرهی is an archaic adjective/noun meaning cultured/noble. The whole phrase is the content of the news (آگهی).
segments.s636-g15: A coordinated noun list: خردمندی (wisdom) و مردی (manliness/valor) و رای (counsel/plan) followed by the possessive pronoun تو placed after the whole series — a common Persian pattern where a trailing pronoun governs the entire coordinated list (i.e., these three qualities belong to ‘you’).
segments.s636-g16: فشرده is a past-participle/adjectival predicate meaning “compressed/condensed/concentrated.” به هرجای = “in every place/wherever,” and بر پای تو literally “on your feet,” an idiomatic locative meaning something like ‘at your command/under your authority.’ The participle acts as the predicate that applies to the three preceding qualities; word order (predicate after list) is typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s636-g49: شهنشاه is the title 'Shahanshah' (king of kings) placed before the name; بهرام is the proper name. گورست is an older/fused form equivalent to 'گور است' (is Gūr) serving as the copula + predicate (here past/statement of identity). و بس is an idiomatic tag 'and that is enough' (و = and, بس = enough / that's all).
segments.s636-g50: چنو here means 'such'/'like that' modifying the implicit comparison; در زمانه is a prepositional phrase 'in the age/era'; ندانیم is the classical present-tense negative 'we do not know' (نده- + انیم), with کس placed after it to mean 'no one'. The word order (adverbial phrase + verb) is typical of poetic/classical Persian.
segments.s636-g51: The repeated preposition به introduces two paired quality-sets: 'به مردی و دانش' = 'in (regard to) manliness and learning' and 'به فر و نژاد' = 'in (regard to) farr (royal glory) and lineage'. Each noun after به names a quality; the conjunction و links the two items in each pair.
segments.s636-g52: چنو 'such' modifies پادشا 'a king' (چنو پادشا = 'a king such as that'). کس ندارد به یاد is an existential/possessive phrasing: 'no one has (him) in memory' i.e. 'no one remembers'. ندارد is third-person singular 'has not'; کس is 'no one' in this negative context; به یاد = 'in memory/remembered'.
segments.s636-g53: جهاندار پیروزگر are two titles/appositive epithets ('world-ruler' and 'victorious') serving as what follows the verb; خواندش is past-tense 'called him' (خواند + enclitic -ش referring to him). Word order places the title(s) before the verb, a normal classical Persian pattern for 'they called him X'.
segments.s636-g54: ز marks the comparison here ('than' / 'of the'), followed by شاهان 'kings'; سرافرازتر is the comparative form of 'sar-afraz' meaning 'more lofty/exalted' (‑تر = more); خواندش again 'they called him'. The whole phrase means 'they called him loftier than kings' or 'they named him loftier than the kings'.
segments.s636-g55: دگر 'the other' (i.e. the other matter) + آنک 'that which/which' introduces the reported thing; گفتی here functions as 'what was said' or 'that you said' (classical usage) and که begins the reported clause 'that I have done'. من کرده‌ام is the perfect 'I have done.' The structure = 'Regarding the other thing that you said I had done...'.
segments.s636-g56: به هندوستان is the prepositional phrase 'to/into India' indicating place; رنجها is the plural object 'hardships/sufferings'; برده‌ام is the first-person perfect 'I have borne/experienced' (برده + ام). The literal order places the place before the object (poetic flexibility), yielding 'I have borne hardships in India.'
segments.s636-g25: به + دستوری here uses به as an instrumental/agent marker ('by'); دستوری is an adjective/noun meaning 'by (the) command'. شاه is the subject 'the king'. در بر گرفت is a compound action: در بر گرفتن = 'to take into one's arms/embrace', with گرفت as the past-tense 3rd-person singular verb. Word order is typical Persian: prepositional phrase ('به دستوری') fronted, then subject (شاه), then the verbal phrase 'در بر گرفت'.
segments.s636-g26: به قنوج شد places the locative/prepositional phrase first: به + place = 'to/into Kannauj', and شد here functions as a simple past occurrence/arrival ('came/was at' — classical Persian often uses شد to mark movement or result). The following یار دیگر گرفت is a coordinated past clause with گرفت (3rd-singular past 'took') and یار دیگر 'another companion' as its object; the subject is the same implied actor as the previous clause.
segments.s636-g27: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb 'now'. رنج بردار is an imperative: رنج (hardship) + بردار (imperative of برداشتن used here = 'bear/take up'), so 'bear the hardship'. و links to the next imperative/command; ایدر بیای is an exhortation 'come here' where بیای is the optative/present-subjunctive form addressed to 'you' ('that you come' / effectively 'come!').
segments.s636-g28: بدین مرز = 'to/at this frontier' (بدین = ب + این, an older fused form meaning 'to/at this'); چندانک is an adverbial 'so long/as long as' or 'to that extent'. باید is the modal 'must/ought to'. به پای (here with بپای later) uses پای and the verb پاییدن: بپای (infinitive/command-like) means 'to remain/abide' (i.e. 'stay'). Overall this clause gives the obligation: 'to this frontier you must come and remain as long as required.' Note: the token 'دین' in the supplied word list corresponds to the fused element -دین (actually the old spelling of 'این'); no sense for 'this' was provided, so it is treated as the demonstrative element.
segments.s636-g29: به دیدار تو is a prepositional phrase 'at/for your sight/meeting' (literally 'to the seeing of you'). چشم روشن کنیم is a poetic idiom: چشم روشن کردن = 'to brighten the eyes' (i.e. to be gladdened); کنیم is 1st-person plural present-subjunctive/optative used as a future or volitional 'we will/may make'. Word order puts the prepositional phrase first for emphasis, then the object idiom and the verb.
segments.s636-g30: روان را is the definite object 'the soul' (marked by را). ز رای تو is an instrumental/causal phrase 'by/from your counsel' (ز = 'by/from' here, رای = 'counsel/plan'). جوشن کنیم = 'we will make (into) armor' (جوشن = armor, metaphorically 'we will arm/fortify'); کنیم again is the 1st-plural optative/volitional. The whole clause means 'we will arm our soul with your counsel.'
segments.s636-g31: چو خواهی که is a conditional 'if you wish that...' (چو = 'if/when' used conditionally here, خواهی = 'you wish/will'). ز ایدر شوی literally 'you become from here' — in context شدن + ز + place = 'to go/leave from here' (شوی is 2nd-singular present-subjunctive). باز جای = 'to another place/elsewhere' (باز modifying جای = 'again/elsewhere'). The clause is a protasis introducing the condition.
segments.s636-g32: زمانی 'for a while' sets the timespan. نگویم here is negative+subjunctive/first-person ('I do not/will not say'); in classical Persian the present-subjunctive form can express what the speaker will or will not do in the future. بر من بپای = 'stay by me' (بر + من = 'at/near me'; بپای from پاییدن = 'remain/abide'). Together: 'I will not say
segments.s636-g73: نباشد is a negative jussive/optative ('may there not be' / 'let there not be'). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. من + بنده functions as the object-like phrase 'my servant' or a humble self-reference ('I, your servant' / 'one of my servants'). همداستان is an adjective/noun meaning 'of the same mind' / 'like-minded'. Together the segment expresses a wish that no servant/agent of mine be like-minded.
segments.s636-g74: که introduces a result/purpose clause ('that / so that'). رانم is راندَن with the enclitic -م = 'drive me' (here with a jussive/optative flavour — 'would drive me' / 'make me'). The tokens ب + دین are split here but form بدین 'in this' (ب- = prefix, دین = archaic demonstrative), meaning 'in this manner'; گونه‌بر ('of such a kind' / 'in such a way') modifies داستان ('tale/story'). Overall: 'that would drive me into this sort of tale.'
segments.s636-g75: دگر is a discourse marker 'the other / moreover'. آنک + گفتی functions as 'that which was said' / 'what you said' (here nominalized). که begins the reported-content clause, and با خواسته means 'with/by request' (i.e. 'at the asking of'). The phrase indicates reference to another point the addressee raised.
segments.s636-g76: به ایران is a prepositional phrase 'to/into Iran'. فرستمت is a past tense verb with the enclitic object -ت meaning 'I sent you' (classical form). آراسته is a postpositive adjective/participial adjective 'adorned', describing the person sent. Word order: prepositional phrase + verb + adjectival complement (object expressed as enclitic).
segments.s636-g77: مرا (here split as م + را) is the patient/object 'me' and کرد is the past 'made' / 'did'. یزدان is the subject 'God' placed after the verb (classical Persian order V–S). ازان بی‌نیاز is a complement meaning 'free/independent from them' (ازان = 'from those/them', بی‌نیاز = 'free of need'). Overall: 'God made me independent of them.'
segments.s636-g78: به چیز کسان is a target phrase 'to the things/belongings of people' (به = toward/to, چیز = 'thing(s)', کسان = 'people'). دست کردن is a verbal construction 'to lay a hand on / to handle / to take', with کردن as the light verb; دراز ('long') modifies دست ('hand') to convey 'a long-reaching hand' or 'a hand that reaches far' (i.e. that takes others' things). The subject is omitted and ties back to the prior clause.
segments.s636-g79: ز here means 'because of / on account of'. بهرام is the proper name 'Bahram' (the cause/benefactor). دارم is the present 'I have' / 'I hold' used to mean 'I owe / I have (thanks)'. به بخشش سپاس: به + بخشش ('for the giving / for his generosity') links the reason, and سپاس is the noun 'thanks/praise'. Together: 'Because of Bahram's generosity I have/owe thanks.'
segments.s636-g80: نیایش کنم is a present/subjunctive construction 'I perform / I should perform / I offer prayer' (کنم is the verb 'to do' with the noun 'نیایش'). روز و شب is the adverbial phrase 'day and night', and در سه پاس specifies the frequency/time 'in the three watches' (پاس = watch/sentry). The verb precedes these time adverbials, which indicate continuous prayer.
segments.s636-g33: برو is an imperative singular 'go' (direct command). شاد is placed after the verb as an adverbial/predicative adjective 'joyfully/happy' modifying the manner of going. با + noun(s) (خلعت و خواسته) is a prepositional phrase 'with the robe and (your) wished-for things'; خواسته here is a noun 'wish/desired thing(s)'.
segments.s636-g34: This phrase continues the description of the subject of برو: خود (here: 'your own / yourself') marks possession/emphasis for preceding noun(s) and links to خواسته; و connects to the next noun; نامداران is a plural adjective/noun 'the renowned/illustrious ones'; آراسته is a predicate/adjectival participle 'adorned' that applies to the subject (you) or to the company, depending on reading (i.e. 'you adorned / surrounded by renowned men').
segments.s636-g35: ترا آمدن is an older dative/accusative pronoun + verbal noun construction meaning 'for you to come' (ترا is historically ت + را). آمدن is the infinitive 'to come'; پیش من is a locative phrase 'in my presence / before me'; ننگ نیست is the nominal predicate 'there is no shame' (ننگ as subject, نیست negating existence). The overall construction expresses 'It is no disgrace for you to come before me.'
segments.s636-g36: چو introduces a causal/temporal clause ('when/since/if'); با شاه ایران is an instrumental/associative phrase 'with the King of Iran' (i.e. 'in relation to the Iranian king'); مرا جنگ نیست is an idiomatic existential phrasing where مرا ('for me') marks the experiencer/dative and جنگ نیست literally 'there is no war' meaning 'I am not at war.' Note that classical Persian often uses مرا for the dative/experiencer role.
segments.s636-g37: مکن is a negative imperative 'do not'. سستی is the noun 'slowness/weakness/laxity' and functions as the object of the prohibition. از آمدن is a prepositional phrase 'in/with respect to coming' (از + infinitive). هیچ رای here means 'for any counsel/opinion/reason' (هیچ = 'any/none', رای = 'counsel/plan') giving the domain of what one must not be lax about.
segments.s636-g38: چو خواهی که is a conditional/complement structure 'if you wish that...' (چو = 'if/when'; خواهی = 'you wish'); برگردی is the present/subjunctive form 'you return / you turn back' governed by the complementizer که. ایدر (اینجا spelled ایدر) is the locative adverb 'here'. مپای is an archaic/poetic negative form (م- prefix + verb پای 'to stay/stand') meaning 'do not stay/linger here.'
segments.s636-g39: چو + past verb (بیامد) yields 'when' (temporal clause). نامه is 'the letter' (subject); بیامد is past 'came/arrived'; به + name is the dative 'to Bahram'; گور here is the epithet 'Gūr' (i.e. Bahram Gūr, 'Bahram the Onager' — a conventional epithet), so the whole phrase means 'when the letter arrived to Bahram Gūr.'
segments.s636-g40: به دلش اندر افتاد is an idiom: به دلش = 'into his heart' (دل + possessive -ش = 'his heart'), اندر افتاد = 'fell into / befell' meaning 'wasfalling into his heart' i.e. 'it stirred him.' زان نامه شور literally 'a passion from that letter' (زان = 'from that/of that', نامه = 'letter', شور = 'passion/ardour'). Word order is poetic: the cause (زان نامه) follows, with شور as the affected thing.
segments.s636-g41: This is two coordinated past-tense clauses. 'نویسنده' is the agent (the scribe). 'بر خواند' is a compound past verb (the prefix بر + خواند) meaning 'read aloud/recited'; the prefix بر is prefixed to the verb rather than standing alone. 'و' links the second clause, and 'پاسخ نوشت' is a simple past clause 'wrote an answer' where 'نوشت' is the past of 'نوشتن'.
segments.s636-g42: Prepositional phrase describing where or how he acted: 'به پالیز' = 'into/into the orchard' (به + noun), 'کین' is the object 'hatred/vengeance', 'بر درختی بکشت' uses an archaic past form 'بکشت' (prefix ب- + root کشت) meaning 'planted' (poetically). 'بر' here is the local 'on/upon' and 'درختی' = 'a tree'. The whole phrase = 'he planted hatred on a tree in the orchard.'
segments.s636-g43: 'سر نامه' = 'the head/heading of the letter' (سر = top/head). 'گفت' is the past of گفتن ('said') and introduces reported content. 'آنچ گفتی رسید' is a clause where 'آنچ' = 'that which', 'گفتی' = 'you said', and 'رسید' = 'has reached/has been received' (past/intransitive perfective). The order is typical: relative content ('that which you said') + verb of attainment ('رسید').
segments.s636-g44: Standard negative-exceptive clause: 'دو چشم تو' ('your two eyes') is the subject; 'جز ... ندید' = 'saw nothing except ...'. 'جز' is the excluding preposition 'except', 'کشور چین' = 'the country/realm of China', and 'ندید' is the past negative 'did not see'. Word order: subject — exceptive phrase — negative verb.
segments.s636-g45: 'به عنوان' is an idiom meaning 'under the title / as the heading'; here 'به' takes an adverbial sense ('by/with') and 'عنوان' = 'title'. 'بر' links the title to what follows ('بر از پادشاه جهان' = 'bearing the form "from the king of the world"'), and 'از پادشاه جهان' literally 'from the king of the world' is the source phrase attached to the title.
segments.s636-g46: 'نوشتی' is 2nd-person past 'you wrote' with the pronoun omitted; it introduces what was written. 'سرافراز و تاج مهان' are epithets/appositive nouns linked by 'و' — 'سرافراز' = 'lofty/honoured' and 'تاج مهان' = 'crown of (the) guests' (here functioning as a title). The whole segment reports the words inscribed under the heading.
segments.s636-g47: 'جز آن بد که گفتی' = 'except that bad thing which you said' where 'که' functions as the relative conjunction. 'سراسر سخن' = 'throughout the speech' or 'in the whole of the words' and stands as a frame for the following judgment. The clause sets up an exception and locates it within the whole utterance.
segments.s636-g48: Word order: object phrase 'بزرگی نو را' ('the new greatness' with را marking it) precedes the verb 'نخواهم' (first-person 'I will not want' / 'I do not wish for'); the final 'کهن' ('the old') is contrastive and elliptical—it implies 'but (I want) the old' though the verb is not repeated. This is a poetic economy: 'I will not want new greatness; (I prefer) the old.'
segments.s636-g17: 'کجا' is the interrogative/adverb 'where' used here rhetorically; it introduces the clause naming the creatures. 'کرگ' is a dialectal bird-name (here 'kerag'/'a bird'), followed by the conjunction 'و' and the demonstrative 'آن' + adjective 'نامور' 'renowned' modifying 'اژدها' 'dragon'. Word order is noun phrase (location-word) + coordinated nouns modified by an adjective phrase.
segments.s636-g18: This is a short predicate clause. 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'. 'شمشیر' 'sword' is modified by the possessive adjective-suffix on 'تیزت' (تیز + ـت) = 'your sharp/keen'. 'نیامد' is the negative past 'did not come' and with the following adjective/participle 'رها' ('loose/free') yields the meaning 'did not become free / was not released.' The verb comes before the predicative adjective in this poetic word order.
segments.s636-g19: 'بتو' is the prepositional phrase 'to you'. 'داد' is the simple past 'gave'. 'دختر' is the direct object 'a daughter'. The relative marker 'که' introduces the clause 'پیوند ماست' — 'پیوند' (bond) followed by the copula 'ست' attached to 'ما' ('our') yields 'who/which is our bond.' Note the relative clause follows immediately after the noun it modifies.
segments.s636-g20: Here 'که' functions as an explanatory/causal 'for/because'. The clause 'هندوستان خاک او را بهاست' has a somewhat marked word order: 'خاک او را' ('her soil', definite/accusative marked with 'را') is the object/theme and 'هندوستان ... بهاست' predicates its value. 'بهاست' is 'is the worth/price'; thus literally 'India is the worth/price of her soil' meaning 'the soil of India is her worth.'
segments.s636-g21: 'سر خویش را' is the object phrase 'his/your own head' (خویش + enclitic ش = 'one's own'; 'را' marks it as definite/accusative). 'بردی' is the 2nd-person past of بردن 'you took/raised'. 'اندر هوا' is a locative/prepositional phrase 'into the air' (اندر = in/into, هوا = air), so the whole segment means 'you raised your head into the air' — idiomatically 'held your head high.'
segments.s636-g22: 'به' here marks instrument or cause ('by/through'). 'پیوند' is 'bond/alliance' and the following noun phrase 'این شاه فرمانروا' (this king sovereign) is its complement: 'the bond with this sovereign king.' The whole segment gives the reason/instrument for the raised head: 'by/through the alliance with this ruler.'
segments.s636-g23: 'به ایران' here expresses the domain/beneficiary ('for/to Iran'). 'بزرگیست' is the fused copula 'is greatness' (بزرگی + است), stating the predicate. 'این شاه را' places 'this king' with the accusative/topic marker 'را' after the predicate for emphasis/wording; together the clause means 'This king is greatness for Iran' (i.e., 'a greatness to Iran').
segments.s636-g24: This is a short interrogative/rhetorical fragment. 'کجا' = 'where' (here rhetorical: 'where is/are ... ?'). 'کهترش' = 'his/its lesser/inferior' (کهتر + enclitic ش). 'افسر' is 'helmet/diadem' (poetically used for a crown). 'ماه' 'moon' modifies 'افسر' (the moon's diadem), and 'را' marks the definite object. The fragment asks rhetorically for anyone whose helmet is inferior compared with the moon's crown.
segments.s636-g81: چهارم is an enumerative adverb 'fourth' that opens the clause. سخن‌گر is the subject 'the speaker/orator'. ستودی is a past tense verb (poetic/archaic ending -ی) meaning 'praised' (3rd-sg in context), and مرا is the object 'me' split here into enclitic م + را (م = object pronoun 'me', را = definite/object marker). Word order is Subject–Verb–Object.
segments.s636-g82: هنر is the noun 'virtue/skill/excellence' as the topic. ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (meaning 'by' / 'because of' here), آنچ = 'that which' (i.e. what was just mentioned), برتر is a comparative 'superior/beyond', and فزودی is a past/poetic verb 'increased/grew' with the enclitic مرا ('me') again marking the experiencer; order is Topic–(prep phrase)–Complement–Verb–object/enclitic.
segments.s636-g83: پذیرفتم is the past first-person singular 'I accepted / I received'. این is the demonstrative 'this' (direct object). از introduces the source 'from', and تو‌ای شاه چین is a vocative 'you, O King of China' (تو with the vocative ای appended to address the addressee). The whole phrase means 'I accepted this from you, O King of China.'
segments.s636-g84: بگوییم is the hortative/subjunctive first-person plural 'let us say/tell' (proposal to act together). با introduces the party/recipient 'with/to', and شاه ایران‌زمین is the noun phrase 'the Shah of the Iranian realm' (the intended addressee of the telling).
segments.s636-g85: ز یزدان is a prepositional phrase 'from God / by God'. ترا is the second-person object 'to you' split here into the enclitic ت + را (ت = 'you' pronoun, را = object marker). باد is the optative particle 'may it be' forming a blessing/wish, and چنان/چندان درود = 'so great/many a greeting/blessing'. The construction expresses a wish: 'May God give you such blessings.'
segments.s636-g86: که introduces a result/consequence clause 'that/so that'. آن را refers back to the blessing just mentioned ('that'), with را as the object/emphatic marker. نداند is third-person (poetic/classical) present or general negative 'does not know' (here: 'cannot comprehend'). فلک تار و پود is a metaphorical phrase 'the heavens' warp and weft' (the very fabric of the sky/fate), meaning even heaven cannot discern the extent of that blessing.
segments.s636-g87: بران is an adverb 'thereupon / upon that'. نامه is the direct object 'letter'. بنهاد is a past-tense verb 'he placed/affixed' (بنهاد = بنهادن, past 3sg). مهر نگین is a compound where مهر functions as 'seal' (here) and نگین 'gem/signet' — 'a gem seal' affixed to the letter.
segments.s636-g88: فرستاد is past third-person singular 'he sent'. پاسخ is the object 'the reply/answer'. سوی is the preposition 'toward/to' indicating the recipient, and شاه چین names that recipient 'the King of China'. The clause is a simple Past (S–V–O with a directional phrase).
segments.s636-g65: This clause is a simple nominal sentence with an indefinite introducer. یکی (an indefinite 'a certain') precedes the noun پادشا (king). بود is the past copula 'was'. شنگل بزرگ is an appositive name/title following the copula (بزرگ is an adjective following the name). Note پادشا is an archaic/formal form of 'king'.
segments.s636-g66: به مردی marks the beneficiary/owner ('for a man' / 'of a man') and is placed before the verb; همی is the archaic progressive particle indicating habitual/continuous action ('would/used to'). راند is the past verb 'drove/expelled'. از introduces the source 'from' (از میش = from the ewe), and گرگ is the direct object 'wolf' placed after the prepositional phrase in this poetic order.
segments.s636-g67: چو = 'when'. با من = 'toward/with me' (here functioning as the object/target of judgement). سزا دید is a classical collocation meaning 'saw (someone) deserving/fit' (دید is past 'saw' and سزا = 'desert/worthiness'). پیوند خویش is the object 'his bond/pledge' with خویش divided into خوی + ش (kinship/possessive) — ش is the 3rd-person possessive suffix.
segments.s636-g68: به من داد = 'gave to me' (به marks the recipient). شایسته is an adjective 'worthy/fit' qualifying what was given; فرزند خویش = 'his child' (فرزند = child, خویش = his, here expressed as two words with خوی + ش). The verb داد is simple past 'gave' and the adjective phrase shایسته modifies the transferred item (he deemed the child suitable for me).
segments.s636-g69: دگر آنک گفتی = 'the other (thing) which you said' (دگر = 'the other/another', آنک = relative 'that/which', گفتی = 'you said' in context). که introduces the reported/quoted speech that follows. خیز and آی are imperatives: 'rise!' and 'come!' and ایدر means 'here'.
segments.s636-g70: به نیکی = 'in goodness' / 'well'. بباشم here is an optative/subjunctive meaning 'may I be' / 'I will be' (classical Persian uses this form after such clauses to express intent or promise). ترا (written as ت + را) is 'to you' with را as the definite/accusative marker attached for emphasis; رهنمای is predicate noun 'guide'. The clause thus states purpose/assurance: 'I will be a good guide to you.'
segments.s636-g71: مرا is the fronted object 'me' (object pronoun fronted for emphasis), here segmented into م + را in the tokenization (م does not match the supplied senses). شاه ایران is the subject 'the king of Iran' (noun + genitive), and فرستد is the past verb 'sent'. به هند is the destination prepositional phrase 'to India'. This word order (object before subject) is common in older Persian narrative.
segments.s636-g72: به چین آیم = 'I came/went to China' (آیم here denotes movement 'I came/went' in the narrative). از بهر is a classical purposive phrase meaning 'for the sake of'/'for' (از used here in the purposive sense together with بهر). چینی modifies پرند: 'Chinese birds' (چینی = Chinese; پرند = birds). The clause expresses purpose: 'I went to China for Chinese birds.'

sentences.s636-s1: When this news came to the Faghfur of China — that a man of farr was from the land of Iran.
sentences.s636-s2: Someone had been sent to the vicinity of Shangal; verily he was a Tahm‑zadeh from Iran.
sentences.s636-s3: To him Shangal gave one of his daughters, whose diadem upon her shone like the moon.
sentences.s636-s4: That sovereign, with his court, wrote a letter to Bahramshah.
sentences.s636-s29: That same star was King Bahrām, who possessed farr and Orvand and had a name/renown.
sentences.s636-s30: Skill likewise belongs to the Iranians — and that is enough; they do not have the 'kerag zhiyān' for anyone.
sentences.s636-s31: All are of one heart and God‑knowing; they do not properly give thanks to the star.
sentences.s636-s32: Another (thing): that the king gave his daughter to me; I married her to a man in this way before the court.
sentences.s636-s5: As a title from the sovereign of the world — the chief of the renowned and king of the great.
sentences.s636-s6: To the Persian envoy who came to Kannauj with thirty companions.
sentences.s636-s7: He further said, 'News will come to us that you are a famed, noble man.'
sentences.s636-s8: Your wisdom, manhood, and counsel are concentrated and set at your feet everywhere.
sentences.s636-s25: The Shahanshah was Bahram Gūr, and that is enough — we know of no one like him in that age.
sentences.s636-s26: In manliness and learning, in farr and lineage, no one remembers a king such as that.
sentences.s636-s27: They called him 'world-ruler, the victorious'; they called him loftier than (other) kings.
sentences.s636-s28: As for the other thing you said I had done — I have borne hardships in India.
sentences.s636-s13: By the king's command he took (her) in his arms; he went to Kannauj and took another companion.
sentences.s636-s14: Now endure the hardship and come here; to this frontier you must come and remain as long as necessary.
sentences.s636-s15: At the sight of you our eyes will brighten; by your counsel we will gird/arm our souls.
sentences.s636-s16: If you wish to depart from here again to another place, I will not say 'stay with me for a while.'
sentences.s636-s37: May none of my servants be of one mind so as to drive me into telling such a tale.
sentences.s636-s38: As for the other thing you said — that, at the request, I sent you to Iran, adorned.
sentences.s636-s39: God made me independent of them; (I) extend my hand to the things of people (i.e. take what belongs to others).
sentences.s636-s40: Because of Bahram's generosity I owe thanks; I offer prayers of thanks day and night in the three watches.
sentences.s636-s17: Go joyfully, wearing the robe of honour and your chosen gifts, adorned with illustrious men.
sentences.s636-s18: It is no shame for you to come before me, since I am not at war with the King of Iran.
sentences.s636-s19: Do not show any slackness in coming for any counsel; if you wish to turn back, do not linger here.
sentences.s636-s20: When the letter came to Bahram Gur, a passion from that letter fell into his heart.
sentences.s636-s21: The scribe read it aloud and wrote a reply; he planted hatred in the orchard on a tree.
sentences.s636-s22: The heading of the letter said: 'What you said has arrived; your two eyes have seen nothing but the realm of China.'
sentences.s636-s23: As a heading you wrote 'From the king of the world'; you inscribed: 'honoured (saraafraz) and crown of guests.'
sentences.s636-s24: Except for that bad thing you said, throughout the speech I will not desire new greatness — (I will prefer) the old.
sentences.s636-s9: Where were the kerag and that famed dragon that did not come free from your keen sword?
sentences.s636-s10: He gave you a daughter who is the bond between us, for the soil of India is her worth.
sentences.s636-s11: You held your head aloft into the air by the bond with this sovereign king.
sentences.s636-s12: This king is greatness to Iran; where is anyone whose helmet is inferior to the moon's diadem?
sentences.s636-s41: Fourth, the speaker praised me; my excellence increased beyond that.
sentences.s636-s42: I accepted this from you, O King of China; let us tell it to the Shah of the land of Iran.
sentences.s636-s43: May God grant you so many greetings that the heavens do not know their warp and weft.
sentences.s636-s44: Thereupon he placed a seal–gem on the letter and sent the reply to the King of China.
sentences.s636-s33: There was a certain king — Shangal the Great — who would drive a wolf away from a man's ewe.
sentences.s636-s34: When he judged me worthy, he gave me his pledge and bestowed upon me a fitting child of his.
sentences.s636-s35: As for the other thing you said — 'Rise up, come here' — I will be a good guide to you.
sentences.s636-s36: The king of Iran sent me to India; I went to China for Chinese birds.
